coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent carbon-dots-based mitochondria-targetable nanoprobe for peroxynitrite sensing in living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Xiaoxue
Sun, Shan
Wang, Yuhui
Zhu, Jiali
Jiang, Kai
Leng, Yumin
Shu, Qinghai
Lin, Hengwei
coredata.dc:description: Mitochondria, the power generators in cell, are a primary organelle of oxygen consumption and a main source of reactive oxygen/nitrogen species (ROS/RNS). Peroxynitrite (ONOO−), known as a kind of RNS, has been considered to be a significant factor in many cell-related biological processes, and there is great desire to develop fluorescent probes that can sensitively and selectively detect peroxynitrite in living cells. Herein, we developed a fluorescent carbon-dots (C-dots) based mitochondria-targetable nanoprobe with high sensitivity and selectivity for peroxynitrite sensing in living cells. The C-dots with its surface rich in amino groups was synthesized using o-phenylenediamine as carbon precursor, and it could be covalently conjugated with a mitochondria-targeting moiety, i.e. triphenylphosphonium (TPP). In the presence of peroxynitrite, the fluorescence of the constructed nanoprobe (C-dots-TPP) was efficiently quenched via a mechanism of photoinduced electron transfer (PET). The nanoprobe exhibited relatively high sensitivity (limit of detection: 13.5nM) and selectivity towards peroxynitrite in aqueous buffer. The performance of the nanoprobe for fluorescence imaging of peroxynitrite in mitochondria was investigated. The results demonstrated that the nanoprobe showed fine mitochondria-targeting ability and imaging contrast towards peroxynitrite in living cells. We anticipate that the proposed nanoprobe will provide a facile tool to explore the role played by peroxynitrite in cytobiology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-dots
Mitochondria-targeting
Fluorescence imaging
Nanoprobe
Peroxynitrite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ONOO− sensing in buffer solution and biological fluids
Sensor response to the other ROS/RNS and metal ions or molecules
Fluorescence imaging of peroxynitrite in living cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of the nanoprobe (C-dots-TPP). (a) FT-IR spectra of the C-dots (black line) and the C-dots-TPP (red line). (b)XPS of the nanoprobe. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots (black line) and the nanoprobe (red line). (d) TEM image of the nanoprobe (inset: diameter distribution). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the nanoprobe (1.0μg/mL) upon the addition of various amounts of ONOO−(0–10µM) in PBS buffer solution (100mM, pH 7.4).(b) A relation curve of fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe and the concentration of ONOO−. (c): A linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe and ONOO− concentration ranging from 0.2 to 1.0mM, Inset: The equation of the linear fitting. The error bars in (b) represents three independent measurements.

(a) Relevant reactions of ONOO−. (b) Schematic illustration of preparation of the mitochondria-targetable nanoprobe and a suggested photoinduced electron transfer mechanism of fluorescence quenching of the nanoprobe in the presence of ONOO−(not to real scale).

Confocal fluorescence images for MCF-7 cells co-stained with nanoprobe (50μg/mL) and Mito-tracker (2.5μg/mL). The cells were excited at 405nm and 633nm, respectively. And the fluorescence images were collected at 500−650nm (a, for nanoprobe) and 650−800nm (b, for mito-tracker), respectively. (c) Merged images. (d) Bright filed image. (e) Fluorescence intensity profile of linear region of interest across a selected single cell. Scale bar: 20µm.

Confocal fluorescence imaging of MCF-7 cells. (a) Cells were incubated with the nanoprobe (control, 25μg/mL), (b) and (c) are corresponding bright field and merged images. (d) Cells were firstly incubated with the C-dots (25μg/mL) and then treated with lipopolysaccharide (LPS, 1µg/mL) and interferon-γ (IFN-γ, 100ng/mL), and (e) and (f) are the corresponding bright field and merged images. The excitation wavelength was 405nm, and the fluorescence images were collected from 500 to 700nm. Scale bar: 20µm.
Comparison of some recently published probes for the detection of ONOO–.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent carbon-dots-based mitochondria-targetable nanoprobe for peroxynitrite sensing in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 10081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology & Engineering (NIMTE), Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology & Engineering (NIMTE), Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mitochondria, the power generators in cell, are a primary organelle of oxygen consumption and a main source of reactive oxygen/nitrogen species (ROS/RNS). Peroxynitrite (ONOO−), known as a kind of RNS, has been considered to be a significant factor in many cell-related biological processes, and there is great desire to develop fluorescent probes that can sensitively and selectively detect peroxynitrite in living cells. Herein, we developed a fluorescent carbon-dots (C-dots) based mitochondria-targetable nanoprobe with high sensitivity and selectivity for peroxynitrite sensing in living cells. The C-dots with its surface rich in amino groups was synthesized using o-phenylenediamine as carbon precursor, and it could be covalently conjugated with a mitochondria-targeting moiety, i.e. triphenylphosphonium (TPP). In the presence of peroxynitrite, the fluorescence of the constructed nanoprobe (C-dots-TPP) was efficiently quenched via a mechanism of photoinduced electron transfer (PET). The nanoprobe exhibited relatively high sensitivity (limit of detection: 13.5nM) and selectivity towards peroxynitrite in aqueous buffer. The performance of the nanoprobe for fluorescence imaging of peroxynitrite in mitochondria was investigated. The results demonstrated that the nanoprobe showed fine mitochondria-targeting ability and imaging contrast towards peroxynitrite in living cells. We anticipate that the proposed nanoprobe will provide a facile tool to explore the role played by peroxynitrite in cytobiology.

ce:section.ce:para: Mitochondria are known as the power source of the cell because of their most prominent roles in producing the energy currency (i.e. adenosine triphosphate (ATP)) through respiration (Ernster and Schatz, 1981). In addition, mitochondria also take part in other physiological processes such as redox balance, storage of calcium ions, regulation of cellular metabolism, apoptosis-programmed cell death, and so on (Ow et al., 2008). In the process of the generation of ATP, many kinds of reactive oxygen/nitrogen species (ROS/RNS) also released in the processing of oxygen to digest carbohydrates and fats (Lin and Beal, 2006). More specially, mitochondria are also involved in the event of ROS/RNS-induced apoptosis and mitophagy (Vafai and Mootha, 2012). Owing to these crucial functions, oxidative damage to the mitochondria plays a significant role in cell death that leading to many neurodegenerative diseases and pathologies including cardiovascular disease, inflammation, osteoporosis and Alzheimer's disease (Finkel et al., 2007). Peroxynitrite (ONOO−) performs as a highly reactive nitrogen species in vivo, which is specially generated in a diffusion-dependant reaction between nitric oxide (NO) and superoxide (Bruemmer et al., 2014; Sun et al., 2015). Due to its high oxidation and nitration capabilities, peroxynitrite is extremely harmful and could damage many intracellular biomolecules including DNA and proteins, and has been paid much more attention in biology (Peng et al., 2014; Pacher et al., 2007). Previous studies have shown that the abnormal levels of peroxynitrite in human body can induce a variety of diseases (Wiseman and Halliwell, 1996; Schieke et al., 1999). Given the detrimental effects of peroxynitrite, it is meaningful and important to develop highly selective and sensitive probes for peroxynitrite sensing, especially in mitochondria. Besides, due to the very short lifetime and relatively low steady-state concentration nature of peroxynitrite (Zhang et al., 2012; Zhou et al., 2014), the achievement of its directly intracellular detection is more practically useful, but this is still a great challenge.

Many techniques have been developed for peroxynitrite detection so far, such as electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy (Yang et al., 2006), UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy (Frankenfeld et al., 2006), electrochemical analysis (Bedioui et al., 2011), and chromatography (Alvarez et al., 1996). Unfortunately, all these approaches suffer from drawbacks of needing complicated sample pre-treatment, utilization of expensive instruments, poor sensitivity/selectivity, and/or unsuitable for intracellular detection. To circumvent these above-mentioned issues, fluorescence technique, which represents a highly sensitive and non-destructive method, has been also applied for peroxynitrite sensing (Tian et al., 2011; Adegoke and Nyokong, 2013; Chen et al., 2015). However, the reported organic fluorescent dye-based probes meet limitations of low photostability, complicated synthesis procedure, and frequently poor water solubility (Wang et al., 2014a, 2014b); meanwhile, the inorganic and nanocrystal-based fluorescent probes are commonly toxic due to containing heavy metal ions, and thus they are not welcome in biologically relevant detection (Tsay and Michalet, 2005). Furthermore, these developed fluorescent probes are still not capable for mitochondria-targetable sensing of ONOO−.

As a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (C-dots) have attracted much attention in recent years due to their many fascinating merits, such as high photostability, excellent biocompatibility, water solubility, and facile preparation (Lim et al., 2015; Ding et al., 2014). Consequently, C-dots have demonstrated many potential applications including chemo-/bio-sensing, bioimaging, biomedicine and optoelectronic devices (Liu et al., 2015; Zheng et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015; Pan et al., 2015; Jiang et al., 2015a). Notably, the superior biocompatibility of C-dots makes them especially appropriate in biologically relevant applications. More recently, phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped C-dots had been synthesized and developed for peroxynitrite sensing (Simoes et al., 2014; Gong et al., 2016). However, their C-dots-based probes are poor in selectivity and sensitivity, thus being unsuitable for intracellular detection.

Herein, in this study, we construct a mitochondria-targetable nanoprobe based on TPP modified carbon-dots (C-dots-TPP). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example on C-dots-based nanoprobe for mitochondrial peroxynitrite detection. The fluorescence emission of the nanoprobe is greatly suppressed in the presence of peroxynitrite due to the process of photoinduced electron transfer. Owing to the excellent optical properties, the proposed nanoprobe exhibits highly sensitive response towardsperoxynitrite. In addition, •NO2 is a kind of free radical intermediate decomposing from ONOO−, and which could specially react with o-diaminobenzene structure on the surface of C-dots to form benzotriazole compounds (Sun et al., 2014; Dong et al., 2014). Therefore, the proposed nanoprobe also shows good selectivity to peroxynitrite. More importantly, this nanoprobe has been verified to be applicable for peroxynitrite detection in mitochondria thanks to its superior biocompatibility and good targeting ability.

All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used as received without further purification. o-phenylenediamine was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethanol, methanol, methylene chloride, NaCl, Na2HPO4, MgSO4, Ca(NO3)2, NaNO2, NaOH, NaH2PO4, KCl, FeCl3, N, N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), 1-hydroxybenzotriazole (HOBT), L-ascorbic acid (AA) and glucose were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). (3-Carboxyprop-1-yl) (triphenyl) phosphonium bromide (TPP-COOH) was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. L-cysteine (Cys), and glutathione (GSH), MnO2, NaNO3, tetrachloro-p-benzoquinone, H2O2, sodium nitroferricyanide (III) dehydrate (SNP) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). A commercially available mitochondria-specific staining dye (Mito Tracker Deep Red) was obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Deionized water was used for the preparation of buffer solution in this study.

The sizes and morphologies of C-dots were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a TecnaiF20 microscope operated at 200kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) was performed on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a PERSEET10CS UV–Vis spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an AXIS ULTRA DLD spectrograph with Al/Kα as the source. Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-4600 spectrophotometer at room temperature. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of the C-dots was determined by a Fluoromax-4 measurement system (HORIBA, JobinYvon. Inc). MTT assay was performed on a microplate reader (Imark 168-1130, Biorad, USA). Cell fluorescence image was observed on a confocal laser fluorescence microscopy (TCSSPSII, Leica, Germany).

Carbon-dots were synthesized in a solvothermal method as described in our previous report (Jiang et al., 2015b). In brief, o-phenylenediamine (0.90g) was firstly dissolved in 90mL ethanol, and then the solution was transferred into poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave. After heating at 180°C in oven for 12h, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally. And then, the solvent in the mixture was removed by evaporation and finally yielded a black solid. Thereafter, the solid was purified with a silica column chromatography using mixtures of methylene chloride and methanol as eluent. After removing solvents and further drying under vacuum, the C-dots were harvested with a yield of ca. 15wt%.

The C-dots with their surface rich in amine were covalently modified with TPP-COOH according to the standard DCC protocol (Mao et al., 2014). In brief, 20mg TPP-COOH was mixed and stirred with DCC (9.6mg), HOBT (7.9mg) in CH3OH. After 1h reaction, C-dots (20mg) were added into the mixture and stirred for 18h. And then, a raw product was obtained after complete evaporation of solvent. Finally, the product was purified by column chromatography using chloroform/methanol (50:1) as eluent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ONOO− sensing in buffer solution and biological fluids
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of ONOO−, various amounts of ONOO−(0–10µM) were added into the PBS buffer solution (100mM, pH 7.4) containing the nanoprobe with a fixed concentration of 1μg/mL. And then, fluorescence emission spectra of the samples were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer under ambient condition. For ONOO- detection in serum and cell culture medium, bovine serum with 40-fold dilution and growing medium were then selected as the assay medium, respectively. An identical detection procedure as in aqueous solution was followed for ONOO-. All the fluorescence spectra were measured under the excitation at 420nm, and the emission intensity at 575nm was recorded for quantitative analysis.

To verify the selectivity, the effects of inspected species including other ROS/RNS (10μΜ), metal ions (0.1mM) and some biological molecules (0.1mM) on the nanoprobe were measured. The detailed preparation procedures of ROS/RNS were offered in supplementary material. In brief, the inspected species were firstly added instead of ONOO−, and then the samples were measured by a fluorophotometer. Subsequently, ONOO−(10µM) were added into the above samples, and the fluorescence spectra of the samples were measured again. All the fluorescence spectra were measured under the excitation at 420nm, and the emission intensity at 575nm was recorded for quantitative analysis.

A standard MTT assay was performed to study the cellular toxicity of the nanoprobe. Briefly, 100µL of cells were seeded in a 96-well plate with a concentration of 1×105 cells per mL and permitted to adhere on the surface of plate after an incubation of 24h at 37°C. Subsequently, the culture medium was abandoned and the cells were treated with Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) including various amounts of the nanoprobe (10–50μg/mL) for one day. Thereafter, the culture medium in the plate was discarded. Subsequently, 10µL of MTT (5.0mg/mL in PBS) and a fresh growing medium (100µL) were added into each well, respectively. After an incubation of 4h, the growth medium was removed and a solvent of DMSO (100µL) was added to dissolve MTT. Finally, the absorption intensity of each sample was recorded through a microplate reader.

To confirm the mitochondria-targetable property of the nanoprobe, mito-tracker was selected as a contrast and co-stained with the nanoprobe. The mito-tracker (2.5μg/mL) was incubated with MCF-7 cells for 2h, and then washed with PBS buffer. After that, the cells were incubated with nanoprobe (50μg/mL) for 10h, and also washed with PBS buffer to remove free nanoprobe. Subsequently, the sample was inspected by a confocal laser fluorescence microscopy.

The application of the nanoprobe for cellular ONOO− imaging was tested by the use of MCF-7, HepG2 and 4T1 cancer cells, respectively. In brief, 2.0mL of the above-mentioned cells in DMEM medium at an initial density of 4×104 cell per mL were seeded in each dish and cultured at 37°C for 24h under a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. The nanoprobe with a concentration of 25μg/mL was prepared in DMEM medium and incubated with the MCF-7 cells for 6h, and then washed three times with PBS buffer to remove the free nanoprobe. Thereafter, interferon-γ (IFN-γ, 1μg/mL) and lipopolysaccharide (LPS, 100ng/mL) were added into the cells and incubated with another 6h. Finally, the samples were observed with confocal laser fluorescence microscopy.

The size and morphology of the as-prepared C-dots was characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. S1a, the TEM image shows that the as-prepared C-dots have good monodispersity with size distribution ca. 3–8nm (Fig. S1b), and this observation also confirms that the C-dots were successfully obtained. Next, photophysical properties of the C-dots including UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence emission/excitation and quantum yield (QY) are individually measured. As shown in Fig S1c, the C-dots have two typical absorption bands centered at ca. 270nm and 420nm, which can be assigned to aromatic π−π* of the C=C and n−π* of C−N transitions (Wang and Zhou, 2014); the optimum excitation and emission wavelengths are 420nm and 575nm, respectively; and the QY of the C-dots is determined to be 13.4%. The emission mechanisms for C-dots are still unclear. Generally, the fluorescence mechanisms of C-dots are considered composing of carbogenic core state emission (excitation-dependence) and surface molecular state emission (excitation- independence) (Zhu et al., 2012; Lim et al., 2015). As in our case, the C-dots are observed to have an excitation wavelength-independent property (Fig. S1d), which has also been discovered in many previous reports and might be attributed to the surface state/defect-dominated emission mechanism (Ding et al., 2016; Cayuela et al., 2016; Dong et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2015). In Fig. S1e and S1f, compared with the fluorescence emission and the absorption spectra of the freshly-prepared C-dots, the stored C-dots (two months later) show no obvious alterations, which effectively demonstrates the long term stability of the C-dots.

To demonstrate the linking between C-dots and TPP, FT-IR and XPS measurements were carried out. As seen in Fig. 1a, the absorption peaks at 1643 (C=O, stretching vibration) and 3170cm−1 (N–H, stretching vibration) of the nanoprobe indicate the formation of acylamide (−HN−C=O). In addition, the unique and relatively strong peak at around 754cm−1 is a typical absorption of C–H (bending vibration) in benzene ring coming from TPP. Furthermore, relatively elemental composition of the C-dots-TPP (C: 78.4%, O: 13.2%, N: 7.4% and P: 1%) was determined by XPS measurement (Fig. 1b). The above results clearly verify the successful conjugation of TPP on the surface of C-dots. In Fig. 1c and d, it is obvious that the morphology, size distribution and fluorescence emission of the C-dots-TPP shows only slight alteration. Thereafter, the stability of the nanoprobe is examined and it reveals excellent photostability under continuous irradiation with ultraviolet light (Fig. S2), nearly constant emission at pH value ranges between 6 and 11 (Fig. S3), and high ionic strengths up to 400mM of NaCl (Fig. S4). Such a stable optical property makes the nanoprobe more suitable for the followed fluorescence sensing.

Subsequently, the as-prepared nanoprobe is investigated for peroxynitrite sensing in aqueous buffer. As shown in Fig. 2a, fluorescence of the nanoprobe is gradually quenched along with the addition of peroxynitrite from 0 to 10μΜ. Then, a relationship between fluorescence intensities of the nanoprobe and the added concentrations of peroxynitrite was plotted (Fig. 2b). It can be seen clearly from this figure that the emission intensities quickly decrease upon addition of peroxynitrite, as well as offering a linear relation with the concentration ranging from 0.2 to 1.0μΜ (inset in Fig. 2b). Based on the linear fitting (Fig. 2c), a limit of detection (LOD) to be of 13.5nM can be obtained according to the standard 3sb/m criterion, where m is the slope for the range of the linearity used and sb is the standard deviation of the blank (Mao et al., 2012). In order to verify the practicability of this nanoprobe, a peroxynitrite titration experiment was performed in complex biological matrix i.e. bovine serum (40-times diluted). As shown in Fig. S5a, fluorescence of the nanoprobe significantly quenches with the addition of ONOO–. And a standard curve similar with that obtained in aqueous buffer also is harvested (Fig. S5b). Furthermore, to prove the accuracy of the nanoprobe, a standard method (UV–vis absorption spectroscopy) was provided to compare with the proposed method (Fig. S6). As shown in Table S1, the concentrations of five inspected samples determined by this nanoprobe approach with that obtained by the standard UV–vis absorption method. These results demonstrate that the nanoprobe shows high sensitivity and good accuracy for ONOO− determination both in aqueous buffer and serum.

A possible sensing mechanism to peroxynitrite with fluorescence quenching of the nanoprobe is proposed as follows. It had been reported that ONOO− could be protonated and formed peroxynitrous acid (pKa=6.8) at neutral pH (reaction 1 in Fig. 3a, in the absence of scavengers), and which then decomposes into nitrate NO3 − and free radical intermediates(reaction 2 in Fig. 3a) such as •OH and •NO2 (Zielonka et al., 2010; Lin et al., 2011). The C-dots with o-diaminobenzene structure on its surface might react with •NO2 selectively to form benzotriazole structures (Sun et al., 2014; Dong et al., 2014). Besides, no obvious alteration of the fluorescence lifetime of the nanoprobe is observed either in the absence or presence of ONOO− (Fig. S7), further supporting the electron transfer based steady quenching mechanism (Barati et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2014a, 2014b). Consequently, we speculate that the fluorescence quenching of the C-dots is mainly attributed to a photoinduced electron transfer process from the excited state electron of C-dots to benzotriazole structure on its surface (Fig. 3b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensor response to the other ROS/RNS and metal ions or molecules
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate selectivity of the C-dots-based nanoprobe to peroxynitrite, a diverse range of potential interferents including reactive oxygen species (ROS) (e.g. O2 –, •OH, H2O2, and 1O2), RNS (e.g. NO), metal ions (e.g. Fe3+, Mg2+ and K+), and some important physiological small molecules (e.g. glutathione, cysteine, aspartic acid, and glucose) are selected to investigate. As shown in Fig. S8, no obvious fluorescence responses are induced with the addition of all the above interfering substances (black bar), while the fluorescence emissions of all the above samples greatly quench upon the subsequent addition of ONOO– (red bar). These findings demonstrate excellent specificity of the nanoprobe towards peroxynitrite. In Table 1, a detailed comparison on analytical performance between the reported probes and this current nanoprobe is provided. As shown therein, the as-proposed nanoprobe exhibits a competitive sensitivity towards ONOO–, which is mainly attributed to the excellent optical properties of the C-dots, e.g. high quantum yield and good photostability. In addition, the proposed nanoprobe also shows excellent selectivity and can be applied for mitochondria-targetable sensing of ONOO–.

Bestowed with good analytical performance (high sensitivity and selectivity), the nanoprobe is proposed to detect ONOO − in living cells. To be applicable in biological samples, materials must be highly biocompatible. Hence, cytotoxicity of the nanoprobe is first taken into consideration by the standard MTT assay. As shown in Fig. S9, after incubating MCF-7 cells with the nanoprobe (10–50μg/mL) for 24h, the cell viability is quite good (above 90%). The results reveal the excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of the nanoprobe.

To demonstrate whether the nanoprobe can target and selectively stain mitochondria, a commercially available mitochondria-specific staining fluorescent dye, i.e. mito-tracker, is applied to co-stain MCF-7 cells with the nanoprobe. The confocal fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells co-stained by the nanoprobe and mito-tracker are shown in Fig. 4. Through comparing the location of the observed red fluorescence from mito-tracker and the yellow fluorescence from nanoprobe, it can be easily found that there is obvious overlap between them. The nanoprobe specifically stains the region of mitochondria, which is mainly attributed to the successful modification of TPP, an admitted mitochondria-targeting moiety in many reports (Zhou et al., 2013; Zhuang et al., 2014; Du et al., 2014). As seen from the merged image (Fig. 4c), the region of yellow emission in cell matches well with that of the red fluorescence, which also suggests that the nanoprobe primarily distributes in mitochondria. The changes in the fluorescence emission intensity profile of linear regions of interest (nanoprobe and mito-tracker) tend to be in synchrony (Fig. 4e). Additionally, the nanoprobe also was successfully applied for mitochondria imaging in another two models of cancer cells i.e. HepG2 (Fig. S10) and 4T1 (Fig. S11). The obtained results obviously verify that the obtained nanoprobe exhibits universal and quite well mitochondria-targetable property towards many kinds of cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of peroxynitrite in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Before the imaging application in cells, the effect of cell culture medium on the nanoprobe is investigated. As shown in Fig. S12, the fluorescence of the nanoprobe dissolved in growth medium gradually decreases with the addition of ONOO– from 4 to 50µM, and reached a platform when further increasing the concentration of ONOO–. Compared with the ONOO– titration in buffer, the fluorescence of the nanoprobe reaches a platform in the presence of a higher concentration of ONOO–, which is might attributed to the existing of many reducing species e.g. protein and sulhydryl compounds (Madigan and Martinko, 2005). This result demonstrates that the nanoprobe also shows robust capacity for peroxynitrite detection in complex biological matrix i.e. cell culture medium. Next, the proposed nanoprobe is attempted to be applied in intracellular peroxynitrite sensing using confocal fluorescence microscopy. MCF-7, a kind of human mammary cancer cell, is selected as the testing model, and which can be stimulated by interferon-γ (IFN-γ) and lipopolysaccharide (LPS) to generate peroxynitrite (Salonen et al., 2006). MCF-7 cells are firstly incubated with the nanoprobe, and then treated with IFN-γ/LPS to activate and produce peroxynitrite. It can be seen clearly that the nanoprobe shows intense fluorescence in MCF-7 cells ( Fig. 5a), but significant quenching occurred after treatment with LPS/IFN-γ (Fig. 5d). Furthermore, the nanoprobe is used for ONOO − sensing in another two cells (HepG2 and 4T1). As seen in Figs. S13 and S14, the fluorescence of the nanoprobe greatly quenches in the presence of LPS/IFN-γ. These results demonstrate that the nanoprobe is applicable for visually detection of ONOO − in mitochondria. To date, few probes have been developed for mitochondria-targetable sensing of ONOO − . More recently, a reaction-active organic probe has been reported for ONOO − sensing in mitochondria with high selectivity and sensitivity (Zhang et al., 2015). Compared with this organic probe, our C-dots-based nanoprobe shows better biocompatibility, photostability, and can be easily synthesized. The above mentioned merits make the nanoprobe much more suitable for biological application, e.g. long-time imaging.

In conclusion, a C-dots-based nanoprobe for the highly selective and sensitive detection of peroxynitrite in mitochondria is reported. The sensing mechanism was discussed and tentatively attributed to a photoinduced electron transfer process. More interestingly, the superior biocompatibility nature of the C-dots and their high selectivity and sensitivity to peroxynitrite makes them applicable for intracellular detection. This work is considered to open up new opportunities for applications in clinical diagnosis, e.g. potential indication of cardiovascular disease and inflammation of peroxynitrite using this nanoprobe. In addition, this study clearly shows that C-dots can serve as a promising nanocarrier for fabricating other organelle-targetable nanoprobes through linking different targeting molecules like aptamers or peptides to C-dots. Nonetheless, two negative issues of the proposed nanoprobe should be mentioned. One is that it was built on the basis of fluorescence quenching (i.e. signal turn-off). The other is that the nanoprobe is not capable to sense peroxynitrite in deep tissues or in vivo due to its visible-light emission property. To overcome these limitations, the ongoing work in our lab is now building nanoprobes with signal turn-on using near-infrared emissive nanomaterials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (21277149, 21305152 and 21507005), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation (LR13B050001 and LY16B050005), and Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau (2014A610195, 2014B82010, 2015A610277 and 2016C50009).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.10.060.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
